title: Small, Cheap, and Full of Teeth: Baby Tyrannosaurs Were Plentiful and Precocious
gist: A study in the journal Biology, built on the first identified hatchling tyrannosaur fossils, finds that baby tyrannosaurs were tiny, roughly housecat-sized, offering new evidence about the species' early life stages and reproductive strategy.

insights.claim: A new study published in the journal Biology presents the first identified fossil remains of hatchling tyrannosaurs, offering direct evidence about the species' early life stages and reproductive strategy.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A genuine fossil-record first that opens a new empirical basis for modeling dinosaur reproductive strategy more broadly.
insights.locator: A new study published in the journal Biology

insights.claim: University of Bath paleontologist Nick Longrich and colleagues identified the hatchling tyrannosaur remains by reviewing existing museum collections rather than through new excavation, suggesting other overlooked juvenile dinosaur fossils may already sit unrecognized in museum archives.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Implies a low-cost research strategy, re-examining existing museum collections, could yield further paleontological discoveries without new digs.
insights.quote: Going through museum collections, my colleagues and I have discovered the first remains of hatchling tyrannosaurs
insights.locator: Going through museum collections, my colleagues and I

insights.claim: The hatchling bones were heavily vascularized, a feature the researchers used to confirm the fossils belonged to immature juvenile dinosaurs rather than small adult specimens.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Documents the specific diagnostic method, bone vascularization, used to distinguish juveniles from small adults, a reusable technique for classifying other ambiguous fossil finds.
insights.locator: The fragmented bones they found were heavily vascularized

insights.claim: Baby tyrannosaurs are estimated to have been about 2.5 feet long and weighed roughly 5.5 pounds, possibly only 3.7 pounds at hatching, smaller than an average adult housecat despite growing into some of the largest land predators ever, such as T. rex and Gorgosaurus.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Quantifies the enormous size gap between hatchling and adult tyrannosaurs, framing the scale of growth these predators underwent.
